content.full_text:
City of

FRESNO

Rod Anaforian
Member of the City Council




May 1, 1990


Ms. Katherine Donovan
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Donovan:

On April 30, 1990 I had a telephone conversation with Mr. John
Wallace. It is upon his suggestion that I am asking for the Fair
Political Practices Commission (FPPC) to provide a written opinion
regarding a question of conflict of interest.

I am currently in my second four year term as a member of the Fresno
City Council. My private sector employment is with Organon
Pharmaceuticals, an international manufacturer and marketer of
pharmaceuticals, as a territory sales representative and district
trainer.

Representing council district number six, my area is the fastest
growing portion of the City. As a result this district is
frequently faced with contentious land use issues concerning new
development and how to retrofit pre-existing infrastructures. One
such retrofit issue that the City Council will be considering early
in 1991 is a matter of redesigning the surface street pattern in and
around St. Agnes Hospital. Solutions that will be considered
include one (currently favored by the City staff) which will
adversely impact the quality of life for a handful (seven or eight)
of homes adjacent to the hospital campus. During a recent
neighborhood meeting I was asked if a conflict of interest existed
for myself on this issue and if I should, therefore, disqualify
myself from future discussions on this matter.

I bring this question to your office for an opinion. To assist your
consideration of this matter the following information on my
compensation package with Organon is provided below.


City Hall • Fresno, California 93721 • 209 488-1560

Organon's home office is located at: 375 Mount Pleasant Ave., West Orange, NJ 07052; phone: (800) 631-1253. My immediate field supervisor is Mr. John Ewell who resides at: 8 Elizabeth Lane, Danville, CA 94526; phone (415) 837-0018. Organon is a division of AKZO America, part of a multi-national corporation based Holland.

As an employee of Organon my primary compensation is a salary. In addition to the salary I receive a nominal quarterly stipend as the district trainer. Additionally, I receive a quarterly bonus based on the sales performance in my territory (see attachment). In 1990 I will earn roughly $15,000-$20,000 (gross) in bonus. No single account (hospital, clinic, pharmacy, surgical center etc.) will generate more than $3,000 of that amount.

Before I develop more information on the relationship between myself, the company and hospitals it is useful to understand a little more about how hospitals interface with pharmaceutical companies and the vast majority of the physicians who deliver care in their facilities. To assist in making this understandable I will use St. Agnes Hospital as an example.

St. Agnes, like nearly all hospitals, belongs to a national buying group. St. Agnes is a member of the Holy Cross Health System whose offices are located at: 3606 E. Jefferson Blvd., South Bend, IN 46615. The concept is that through the pooling of their purchasing power the hospitals can negotiate a lower price on many of the durable and non-durable goods necessary for proper hospital operations. Not all products, however, can be acquired at a lower price by such purchase groups. For example, patent protected pharmaceuticals, occasionally, are without competition and thus are insensitive to competitive bidding procedures. (In this particular case, the drug that determines over 90% of my bonus payout, Norcuron is in such a category.)

Additionally, nearly all hospitals purchase pharmaceutical products from regional wholesalers. In this way the hospital is able to keep its inventory levels to a minimum. Moreover, hospitals are thus able to order and receive delivery with 24 hours. St. Agnes acquires its pharmaceuticals in this fashion. (In Fresno only Fresno Community Hospital periodically acquires the Organon product line directly from Organon.)

As with any sales position, my job is to increase demand and therefore use of my product line. To do this I make sales calls and provide support service to those who would have need of Organon's products. In the case of Organon's product line and sales emphasis, this means that I call on anesthesiologists (M.D.) and anesthetists (C.R.N.A.).

The anesthesiologists at non-public hospitals are not hospital employees. Their services are contracted by the hospital. The Fresno area hospitals do employ, directly, their anesthesia professionals. In Fresno the public institutions are: Veterans Administration Hospital (federal agency) and Valley Medical Center (county agency).

What is the reality of "selling" Norcuron to an anesthesiologist?
If a physician chooses to prescribe Norcuron for a patient who is to
undergo surgery he/she simply secures the drug from the hospital
supply. As that supply diminishes the hospital will reacquire from
an area wholesaler. Thus, while the hospital will make the actual
physical purchase of pharmaceuticals, the physician (a non-hospital
employee) is responsible for determining what pharmaceutical
products are indicated and used in pursuit of optimal care for
patient.

In review then, I market a line of pharmaceutical products offered
by Organon Pharmaceuticals to physicians, not hospitals. In pursuit
of this effort I am compensated with the previously described
package. If a physician opts to prescribe a pharmaceutical from my
line the resultant compensation to me will be the same whether
he/she prescribes the product in Fresno or Madera. The cost of this
patent protected product is the same regardless of source or
location of use and is published in the Red Book (national
publication of pharmaceutical prices). Again, the hospital acts as a
conduit to facilitate the availability of pharmaceuticals for the
benefit of a patient as determined by a physician. The prescribing
physician (a non-hospital employee) is the decision maker.

I understand, from my conversation with Mr. Wallace, that this
opinion may take longer than twenty-one days to prepare. Further, I
understand that additional direct contact with my office may be
required to "fill in the blanks". I look forward to your office's
attention to this request and will be happy to assist in any further
way that I can.

Sincerely,

Rod Anaforian
Councilman

Attachment

1990 BONUS PLAN
* * * * * * * *

Professional Medical Representative

| Category A (3.25) | Category B (2.0) | Category C (1.5) | Category D (1.0) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Norcuron® | *Arduan® | Calderol® Deca-Durabolin® Enzymes | Regonol® Reversol® Wigraine® Pregnyl® Cortrosyn® Durabolin® |

* All sales of Arduan® during 1990 will be combined with Norcuron® for quota purposes.

Payouts

A) Eligible Professional Medical Representatives will receive a 1% payout.

B) Senior Medical Representatives will receive a 1.25% payout.

Definitions: AQ = Actual Quota - Sales objective that should be achieved.
EQ = Earnings Quota - Sales level at which bonus can be earned EQ is calculated at 80% of AQ.

All eligible representatives will receive bonus predicated on their total weighted sales in excess of the assigned quarterly Norcuron®/Arduan® quota (EQ). To qualify, a representative must have Norcuron®/Arduan® sales in excess of 80% (EQ) of quarterly quota (AQ) up to 125%. Bonus will be paid at the actual percentage of quota achieved (118% of quota (AQ) = 118% payout). Those representatives attaining 80% to 84.9% will be paid at 50% of their normal rate. Those attaining 85% to 89.9% will be paid at 75% of their normal rate. (Any representative who has achieved a 70% market share (Matty) will be paid at 100% of normal rate once they achieve 80% (EQ) of quota.)

If any eligible representative fails to make EQ in any given quarter, there will be an opportunity to make up that shortfall at the end of the next quarter if the total of both quarters exceed combined AQ. There will also be an opportunity to make up for missing any quarterly EQ if the total yearly sales surpass yearly AQ.

Bonus Plan - Part B

In the event that Norcuron®/Arduan® sales should exceed 125% of AQ
during a given quarter, the representative will be paid an additional
1% on all weighted Norcuron®/Arduan® sales over 125%.

For Example:

Norcuron® sales                           $140,000
Norcuron® Quota (AQ)                    -100,000
Variance                                  40,000
25%                                       25,000
Premium Sales                             15,000
Norcuron® Weight                            3.5
Weighted Premium                         52,500
Premium Rate                              ÷ 0.1
Premium                                 $ 525.00

Part B is open ended without a ceiling.


Eligibility

A representative becomes eligible for participation in the
Professional Medical Representatives Bonus Plan upon completing Level
III Training and 12 months on the job.

A Professional Medical Representative must complete the full quarter
to be eligible for any bonus during that quarter.

While no changes in this plan are contemplated for 1990, management
reserves the right to make changes when deemed necessary.




APPROVED: ___________________        ____________________
          Alan Cohen                David W. Dingwell
          National Sales Manager     V.P., Marketing/Sales
